summary: Multi-agent platform that verifies and executes cloud infrastructure optimizations.
editor_note: Cloud cost tools usually stop at dashboards; this one wants permission to change the infra. Stratusphere shows AWS/Azure agents, policy simulation, blast-radius analysis, rollback, audit trails, and executive P&L dashboards. Unlike FinOps reporting that leaves engineers with tickets, the bet is verified execution under guardrails. The question is whether enterprises let it close the loop.

product_evidence.claim: The official site describes Stratusphere as a multi-agent platform that observes environments, simulates plans under policy, and executes safe changes across AWS and Azure.
The official product copy shows closed-loop opportunity surfacing, approval, execution, rollback, auditability, blast-radius analysis, and executive dashboards for cloud savings and risk reduction.
The funding announcement says Stratusphere identifies, approves, executes, and verifies cloud infrastructure optimizations.

market_context.primary_user: Cloud platform, FinOps, security, and engineering leaders trying to turn cloud recommendations into safe production changes.
market_context.differentiation: StratusGrid positions the product around policy-safe execution and verification rather than visibility-only optimization dashboards.
market_context.why_now: AI workloads are increasing infrastructure cost and change complexity, making verified cloud optimization a sharper problem than another reporting layer.

risks.claim: Outcome-based cloud automation depends on customers trusting the approval and rollback loop enough to let agents make real infrastructure changes.
risks.basis: VentureDex editorial assessment based on official product evidence and the funding source's emphasis on verified execution.
